origin: Woodlands of Japan, Korea & northeast China
habitat: Shaded forest floors and woodland slopes of temperate East Asia
blurb: Barrenwort, the dry-shade champion: airy sprays of delicate, long-spurred 'bishop's hat' flowers in spring over heart-shaped leaflets that emerge red-bronze. Once established it shrugs off the dry, root-filled shade under trees where almost nothing else will grow.
size: 8–12 in tall × 12 in wide, slowly spreading
water: Low; one of the most drought-tolerant perennials for dry shade once established
spread: Slowly spreading clump (short rhizomes) into a low groundcover; well-behaved
seasons: Heart-shaped leaflets flush red-bronze in spring then green in summer; delicate spurred flowers on wiry stems in spring
wildlife: Minor pollinator value; prized mainly as a tough dry-shade groundcover; deer- and rabbit-resistant
toxic: Non-toxic in the garden; the leaves are a traditional East Asian medicinal herb, not a food
